mnemonics.title: DNS Query Types
mnemonics.content: Recursive: Resolver Does The Heavy Lifting | Iterative: Resolver Just Gives Best Answers Available

acronyms.usage: The Internet's phone book that translates domain names to IP addresses
Complete domain name with trailing dot (e.g., www.example.com.)
Duration in seconds that a DNS record can be cached
Highest level in DNS hierarchy (.com, .org, .edu, etc.)
Protocol to authenticate DNS data and prevent spoofing
DNS record specifying mail servers for a domain
DNS record containing administrative info about the zone
DNS record creating an alias for another domain name

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted tree with root (.) at top, branches as TLDs, and leaves as second-level domains. The root is the trunk, TLDs are major branches, and subdomains are smaller branches.

Think of it like asking for directions: You ask a local guide (resolver) → They ask the main office (root) → Office sends you to district office (TLD) → District sends you to local office (authoritative) → You get the answer.

A = Address (IPv4), AAAA = Address (IPv6, four times bigger), CNAME = Nickname/Alias, MX = Mail Xchange, NS = Name Server, SOA = Start (of authority), PTR = Pointer (reverse), TXT = TeXT (descriptive)

analogies.analogy: DNS is like a phonebook for the Internet. Just as you look up a person's name to find their phone number, DNS looks up a website name to find its IP address.
Like asking a librarian to find a book - they do all the searching and bring you the book directly.
Like asking for directions and being told 'ask the person at the next corner' - each step gives you a better clue but you do more legwork.
Like keeping frequently called phone numbers in your speed dial - you don't need to look them up every time.
